other_observers: None.; I originally found the bird but, as mentioned before, it was later seen 9 July by Coen Dexter and Brenda Wright about 2 miles away.
description: White walking across a field, my attention was first drawn to a song reminiscent to that of a slow Blue Grosbeak. I immediately knew that it was a bird not regular in the area and, as I have recently spent a lot of time in Louisiana and Arizona, was under the impression that it could be a Summer Tanager. Upon rounding a corner, I saw the bird perched in the top of a Fremont Cottonwood, actively singing from a stationary perch. The bird was yellowish all over except for red feathers that were coming in scattered over the body and the head, which was already almost completely red. This confirmed my suspicion of it being a Summer Tanager, and after studying the bird a while and seeing the relatively large-billed appearance and 'difference' in song from Louisiana birds I am used to, I concluded that the bird was most likely an individual of the cooperi population. I have seen nominate rubra in Mesa County before, and this bird definitely had a different structure more similar to those in Arizona.Behaviors: For the entirety of the time of observation, the bird was in the top of a Fremont Cottonwood actively singing. After about four minutes, the bird flew, but could still be heard singing for quite a bit longer as it moved from location to location.Call: Song was a slow whistled series of phrases, reminiscent of an extremely slow Blue Grosbeak more than that of a slow American Robin.Plumage: 1st year
